title: Woven coaster set in plain weave
subtitle: Four square coasters on a rigid heddle or frame loom
excerpt: Four square coasters woven in cotton on a rigid heddle or frame loom. Plain weave at a firm sett produces a dense, absorbent fabric. The project is sized to work on a 30 cm rigid heddle or a small frame loom, and uses the finished warp to produce all four coasters in one sitting.
sourceNotes: Woven coasters are a standard beginner weaving project for rigid heddle and frame looms. The sett and warp length calculations are based on standard cotton DK/worsted sett conventions. Observation-led.

content.text: Four 10 x 10 cm coasters woven in
on cotton yarn. The project uses a 20 cm weaving width at a
of 8 ends per 2.5 cm. All four coasters are woven on one warp, separated by a few rows of waste yarn.

items.name: Cotton yarn, DK or worsted weight, main colour
Cotton yarn, contrasting colour (for stripes if desired)
Rigid heddle loom, 30 cm weaving width, with 7.5 or 10 dent heddle

content.text: Warp to a 20 cm width using the direct warping method (winding from the back peg directly through the heddle slot and hole alternately). Warp length: 120 cm to allow for take-up, loom waste, and all four coasters. Use a 7.5 or 10 dent heddle (or the rigid heddle equivalent that gives 8 ends per 2.5 cm). You will thread approximately 64 warp ends across the 20 cm width.
Wind weft onto the stick shuttle. Raise the heddle (up position) and pass the shuttle through the shed, leaving a 5 cm tail at the

. Beat gently with the heddle and lower it (down position) for the next pick. Weave until the piece measures 10 cm from the start. Check the selvedges: they should be straight, not pulling in. If they pull, arc the weft slightly (leave it in a gentle curve in the shed before beating) rather than pulling it tight.

Weave 3-4 rows in a contrast colour or waste yarn to mark the boundary between coasters. These rows will be removed after cutting apart. Weave the second coaster immediately after, and repeat for coasters three and four.

Cut the warp from the loom leaving 5 cm fringe at each end of the finished piece. Cut apart the coasters at the waste yarn rows. Remove the waste yarn rows. Knot the warp fringe on each coaster in pairs with an overhand knot to secure the weft rows, then trim the fringe to 1 cm or weave the warp ends back in using a blunt needle. Hand wash the coasters in warm water to even the sett, and press flat under a damp cloth to finish.

attrs.intro: Two problems specific to coaster weaving.

items.fix: Arc the weft in the shed before beating. The weft needs extra length to travel over and under each warp thread without drawing the selvedge in.
items.cause: Weft is being pulled too tight at the turn.
items.symptom: Warp pulls in at the sides, making the coaster narrower than 10 cm

items.fix: Beat lighter: the heddle should just touch the fell line, not slam into it. If the sett is very close, re-sleying with a more open dent will help on a future warp.
items.cause: Sett is too close, or the cotton was beaten too firmly.
items.symptom: Coasters are stiff and board-like rather than soft

attrs.heading: Common problems
